summary:
The amino acid glycine does not form acetyl-CoA directly from pyruvate but instead generates succinate within metabolic pathways; this makes it distinct compared to other specified amino acids when considering entrance routes into the Krebs cycle.
The specific biochemical path taken by each amino acid determines whether they can contribute directly towards generating acetyl-CoA necessary step inside krebs cycles – notably glycine diverges significantly using alternative metabolites thus avoiding direct inclusion mechanism described above involving most others presented herein.\ Summary:

Glycine does not enter the Krebs cycle by forming acetyl-CoA because it produces succinate instead. Other amino acids like alanine generate acetyl-CoA after becoming pyruvate, making them part of the standard Krebs cycle pathway.
